enrichment.short_description: Open-source flat-file CMS that turns Markdown files into websites, documentation, and eBooks without a database.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free (open source), with optional paid premium extensions

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Typemill is a lightweight, MIT-licensed CMS built for teams who write in Markdown and need a structured way to publish it. Instead of a relational database, content lives as plain Markdown and YAML files, which makes projects easy to version, back up, and move between servers.

It ships with both a visual block editor and a raw Markdown editor, a media library, multi-project and multi-language support, and role-based user management. Beyond websites, Typemill can export the same content directly to PDF and EPUB, which is why it's frequently used for manuals and handbooks rather than general-purpose blogging.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Typemill is aimed at teams that maintain long-lived reference content: technical documentation, user manuals, internal handbooks, and knowledge bases. Because it's file-based and lightweight, it also suits developers who want a self-hosted, Git-friendly alternative to heavier CMS platforms for informational sites.

features.title: Markdown block editor
features.description: Edit content in a structured block editor or switch to raw Markdown for full control.

features.title: eBook export
features.description: Generate PDF and EPUB files directly from the same Markdown content used for the website.

features.title: Flat-file storage
features.description: Content is stored as Markdown and YAML files rather than a database, simplifying backups and version control.

features.title: Multi-project and multi-language
features.description: Run several documentation projects and language variants from a single installation.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Access and manage content programmatically through a built-in REST API.

features.title: AI provider integration
features.description: Connect Typemill to AI providers such as ChatGPT and Ollama for content assistance.

features.title: Role-based user management
features.description: Configure granular permissions for editors, authors, and administrators.

pros: No database required, which simplifies hosting and backups
Free and open source under the MIT license
Generates PDF/EPUB output alongside the website
REST API for programmatic content access
Lightweight and fast to install via ZIP, GitHub, or Docker
cons: Flat-file architecture is less suited to very large, database-driven sites
Smaller plugin/theme ecosystem than mainstream CMS platforms
Some advanced features are only available as paid premium extensions

faqs.question: Is Typemill free?
faqs.answer: The core CMS is free and open source under the MIT license. Some premium extensions are sold separately.

faqs.question: Does Typemill use a database?
faqs.answer: No. Content is stored as Markdown and YAML files on disk instead of a relational database.

faqs.question: Can Typemill publish eBooks?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can generate PDF and EPUB files from the same content used for the website.

faqs.question: How is Typemill installed?
faqs.answer: It can be installed via a ZIP download, from GitHub, or with Docker on an Apache or Nginx server.

use_cases.title: Technical documentation sites
use_cases.description: Publish versioned, Markdown-based manuals and API docs without managing a database.

use_cases.title: Company handbooks and knowledge bases
use_cases.description: Maintain internal reference material with role-based access control.

use_cases.title: eBook publishing
use_cases.description: Write once in Markdown and export the same content as a website and as a PDF/EPUB.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Typemill is an open-source, self-hosted flat-file CMS for publishing Markdown documentation, manuals, and eBooks without a database.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source PHP CMS with a drag-and-drop page builder, built-in ecommerce, and headless REST/GraphQL APIs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Vvveb is a free, open-source PHP content management system that pairs a visual, WYSIWYG drag-and-drop page builder with an integrated ecommerce module. It supports MySQL, SQLite, and PostgreSQL, and can run in either a traditional server-rendered mode or as a headless backend via REST and GraphQL APIs.

The project emphasizes performance and security out of the box, with caching for fast page loads, randomized admin login paths, SQL injection protection, and two-factor authentication. It also includes multi-site management, letting a single installation power more than one website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Vvveb targets bloggers, small businesses, and online retailers who want a self-hosted, no-code way to build and manage websites and stores, as well as developers who want a hybrid traditional/headless CMS they can self-host on standard PHP hosting.

features.title: Drag-and-drop page builder
features.description: Build and customize pages visually without writing code.

features.title: Built-in ecommerce
features.description: Sell products with an included store module rather than a separate plugin.

features.title: Headless REST and GraphQL APIs
features.description: Use Vvveb as a headless backend for custom frontends.

features.title: Multi-site management
features.description: Run multiple websites from a single Vvveb installation.

features.title: Multi-language and multi-currency
features.description: Publish content and sell products in several languages and currencies.

features.title: Security hardening
features.description: Randomized admin paths, SQL injection protection, integrity checks, and 2FA.

features.title: Theme and plugin marketplace
features.description: Extend the CMS with community themes and plugins.

pros: Free and fully open source
No-code drag-and-drop builder plus headless API option
Ecommerce included without extra plugins
Supports MySQL, SQLite, and PostgreSQL
Built-in security hardening features
cons: Smaller community and ecosystem than WordPress or larger CMS platforms
PHP-based, so it requires PHP hosting knowledge to self-manage
Theme/plugin marketplace is less extensive than mainstream competitors

faqs.question: Is Vvveb CMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Vvveb is free and open source, with its code available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can Vvveb be used as a headless CMS?
faqs.answer: Yes, it exposes REST and GraphQL APIs so it can serve as a headless backend for custom frontends.

faqs.question: Does Vvveb support ecommerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a built-in ecommerce module for selling products.

faqs.question: What databases does Vvveb support?
faqs.answer: MySQL, SQLite, and PostgreSQL.

use_cases.description: Use the REST/GraphQL API to power a custom frontend or app.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Vvveb CMS is a free, open-source PHP website builder with drag-and-drop editing, built-in ecommerce, and headless REST/GraphQL APIs.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source Python CMS built on Django, used by organizations like NASA and Mozilla for content-heavy websites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Wagtail is an open-source content management system built on Django, positioned as a leading Python CMS for organizations that need editorial flexibility combined with developer control. Its StreamField editor lets content teams compose flexible page layouts from reusable blocks, while developers can extend the system using standard Python and Django code.

Wagtail supports both traditional server-rendered sites and headless setups through its API, and offers multi-language publishing via Wagtail Localize and content personalization features. It is maintained by Torchbox with contributions from a large community of over 200 contributors, and is used by organizations including NASA, Google, the NHS, Mozilla, and MIT.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Wagtail suits organizations with in-house Python/Django development capacity that need an enterprise-grade CMS: government agencies, universities, nonprofits, cultural institutions, and larger companies that want full control over their content platform without licensing fees.

features.title: StreamField editor
features.description: Compose flexible page layouts from reusable, structured content blocks.

features.title: Headless/API mode
features.description: Serve content to any frontend through Wagtail's API alongside traditional rendering.

features.title: Multi-language publishing
features.description: Manage translated content across locales via Wagtail Localize.

features.title: Content personalization
features.description: Deliver segmented content to different audiences.

features.title: Built on Django
features.description: Extend the CMS with any Python or Django package, model, or integration.

features.title: Role-based permissions
features.description: Control editorial workflows and access at a granular level.

pros: Free, open source, with no licensing fees
Full power of Python/Django available for customization
Proven at enterprise scale with major institutional adopters
Active development with 200+ contributors
Supports both traditional and headless delivery
cons: Requires Python/Django expertise to set up and extend
Steeper learning curve than simpler flat-file or no-code CMS options
Self-hosting requires managing your own infrastructure and updates

faqs.question: Is Wagtail free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wagtail is free and open source with no licensing fees.

faqs.question: What is Wagtail built on?
faqs.answer: It's built on Django, so any Python/Django code and packages can be used to extend it.

faqs.question: Can Wagtail be used as a headless CMS?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports API-driven headless delivery in addition to traditional server-rendered pages.

faqs.question: Who maintains Wagtail?
faqs.answer: It's maintained by Torchbox with contributions from a large open-source community.

use_cases.description: Run large, content-heavy sites with editorial workflows and personalization.

use_cases.description: Use Wagtail as an API-driven content source for custom frontends or apps.

use_cases.title: Multi-language publishing
use_cases.description: Manage and translate content across multiple locales.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Wagtail is a free, open-source Python CMS built on Django, offering StreamField editing, headless APIs, and enterprise-grade flexibility.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source CMS built on Laravel, forked from October CMS in 2021 to stay community-driven and open.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Winter CMS is a free, open-source CMS built on the Laravel PHP framework. It was created in March 2021 when the former maintainers of October CMS forked the project after October's founders decided to make October CMS a closed, paid product, abandoning its open-source community. Winter CMS was built to remain fully compatible with existing October CMS sites while staying free and MIT-licensed.

The project focuses on speed, simplicity, backwards-compatible stability, and security through full source transparency. Developers can build sites with primarily HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, and extend functionality through a plugin and theme marketplace.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Winter CMS is aimed at developers and agencies who want a Laravel-based CMS with plugin/theme extensibility, ranging from small brochure sites to more complex applications, without the licensing costs or lock-in that other Laravel CMS products introduced.

features.title: Laravel foundation
features.description: Built on the Laravel PHP framework, giving developers access to Laravel's ecosystem and conventions.

features.title: Plugin and theme marketplace
features.description: Extend sites with community-built plugins and themes.

features.title: Backwards compatibility
features.description: Maintains compatibility with existing October CMS projects and plugins.

features.title: Simple front-end development
features.description: Build pages primarily with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

features.title: Open source transparency
features.description: Full source code is public, with active issue resolution by maintainers.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Drop-in compatible with existing October CMS sites
Built on the well-established Laravel framework
Plugin/theme marketplace for extending functionality
Community-driven governance after the October CMS split
cons: Smaller ecosystem than WordPress or Drupal
Requires PHP/Laravel knowledge to develop and self-host
Paid support is only available through a third-party (Ring HQ), not the core team

faqs.question: Is Winter CMS related to October CMS?
faqs.answer: Yes, Winter CMS is a community fork created in 2021 by October CMS's former maintainers after October became a paid, closed product.

faqs.question: Is Winter CMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What framework is Winter CMS built on?
faqs.answer: It's built on the Laravel PHP framework.

faqs.question: Can I migrate an October CMS site to Winter CMS?
faqs.answer: Winter CMS was designed to be backwards-compatible with existing October CMS projects and plugins.

use_cases.description: Build and maintain client sites on a stable, plugin-extensible Laravel CMS.

use_cases.description: Extend a CMS foundation into more complex, application-like sites.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Winter CMS is a free, open-source Laravel CMS forked from October CMS in 2021, offering plugin extensibility and backwards compatibility.

enrichment.short_description: The smallest flat-file CMS: a free, open-source website builder that runs on just five files with no database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: WonderCMS bills itself as the smallest flat-file CMS available, running on just five files with no database or configuration step required — you unzip and upload it to get a working site. Content is stored in a JSON-based data store rather than a relational database, keeping the footprint minimal and installations fast.

It offers inline WYSIWYG editing, where you click directly on page content to edit it, along with a theme system, a plugin architecture, brute-force login protection, automatic backups, and SEO-friendly URLs. The project has been free and open source (MIT licensed) since 2008, and ships without ads, tracking, or 'powered by' links.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: WonderCMS suits individuals and small businesses who want the simplest possible self-hosted website or blog — one that installs in minutes, loads fast, and doesn't require database administration or ongoing maintenance overhead.

features.title: Five-file installation
features.description: No database or configuration; unzip and upload to deploy a working site.

features.title: Inline WYSIWYG editing
features.description: Click directly on live page content to edit it.

features.title: JSON-based data store
features.description: Content and settings are stored in flat JSON files instead of a database.

features.title: Plugin and theme system
features.description: Extend functionality and change appearance with community plugins and themes.

features.title: Automatic backups
features.description: Built-in backup functionality protects site content.

features.title: Brute-force login protection
features.description: Login security hardening is included by default.

pros: Extremely lightweight and fast, with strong PageSpeed scores by default
No database or complex setup required
Free and open source since 2008
No ads, tracking, or attribution links
One-click updates
cons: Not suited to large, content-heavy, or highly dynamic sites
Smaller plugin ecosystem than mainstream CMS platforms
Flat-file storage can be harder to scale for high-traffic sites

faqs.question: Does WonderCMS need a database?
faqs.answer: No, it uses a flat, JSON-based data store instead of a relational database.

faqs.question: Is WonderCMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: How is WonderCMS installed?
faqs.answer: Unzip and upload five files to your server — no configuration step is required.

faqs.question: Does WonderCMS support blogging?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a blog mode alongside standard page publishing.

use_cases.title: Simple personal or small-business sites
use_cases.description: Launch a fast, lightweight website without managing a database.

use_cases.title: Low-maintenance blogging
use_cases.description: Run a blog with minimal ongoing administration.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: WonderCMS is a free, open-source flat-file CMS that runs on five files with no database, offering inline editing and fast performance.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted low-code platform with CRM, case management, workflow automation, and access control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Corteza is an open-source, self-hosted low-code platform originally released by Crust Technology in 2019 and donated to the Commons Conservancy the same year to keep it community-governed. It bundles CRM, case management, and general low-code application building into one platform, with a fine-grained, role-based access control system for defining permissions.

The platform includes workflow automation for building automated processes such as email workflows and record handling, an Integration Gateway for connecting external systems and APIs, and built-in reporting and data discovery tools. It can be self-hosted via Docker or compiled binaries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Corteza is aimed at organizations that want a self-hosted CRM and low-code application platform without vendor lock-in, particularly ones with data sovereignty or security requirements that make hosted CRM SaaS products unsuitable.

features.title: Low-code application building
features.description: Build custom business applications and workflows with minimal custom coding.

features.title: CRM and case management
features.description: Manage customer relationships and case workflows out of the box.

features.title: Fine-grained access control
features.description: Role-based and contextual permissions control who can see and do what.

features.title: Workflow automation
features.description: Automate processes such as email notifications and record handling.

features.title: Integration Gateway
features.description: Connect external systems and APIs into Corteza workflows.

features.title: Federation support
features.description: Synchronize data across multiple Corteza nodes.

pros: Free and open source, self-hosted
Combines CRM, case management, and low-code app building in one platform
Fine-grained, role-based access control
Integration Gateway for connecting external systems
Community-governed via the Commons Conservancy
cons: Requires self-hosting expertise (Docker or compiled binaries)
SQLite in-memory storage is unsuitable for production; PostgreSQL or MySQL recommended
Smaller community than mainstream CRM platforms

faqs.question: Is Corteza free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Corteza is open-source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: What is Corteza used for?
faqs.answer: It's a low-code platform used for CRM, case management, and building custom business applications.

faqs.question: How is Corteza deployed?
faqs.answer: It can be self-hosted using Docker or compiled binaries.

faqs.question: Who maintains Corteza?
faqs.answer: It was originally built by Crust Technology and donated to the Commons Conservancy in 2019 to keep it community-governed.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source CRM built on Django Admin for managing leads, deals, contacts, tasks, and email campaigns.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Django-CRM is a free, open-source customer relationship management system built directly on the Django Admin framework, so Python/Django developers can understand, customize, and extend it without learning a proprietary system. It handles the core CRM workflow — contacts, leads, and deals — alongside task management, sales pipeline stages, and analytics.

The project includes email integration and campaign tools, automatic and semi-automatic lead capture from web forms and incoming emails, Excel import/export for bulk data handling, and REST API endpoints with interactive Swagger documentation for programmatic access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Django-CRM is aimed at small and medium-sized businesses, and particularly Python/Django teams, who want a self-hosted CRM they can customize directly in code rather than through a vendor's proprietary configuration layer, without per-user licensing costs.

features.title: Sales pipeline management
features.description: Define custom sales stages and track deals as they progress.

features.title: Lead capture
features.description: Capture leads automatically from web forms or by parsing incoming emails.

features.title: Task management
features.description: Create and assign tasks to team members for follow-up and collaboration.

features.title: Email marketing and campaigns
features.description: Run email campaigns and keep communication history linked to CRM records.

features.title: CRM analytics
features.description: Generate reports on the sales funnel and team performance.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Programmatic access to CRM data with interactive Swagger documentation.

features.title: Excel import/export
features.description: Bulk-handle companies, contacts, leads, and deals for migrations and backups.

pros: Free and open source, no per-user licensing fees
Built on Django Admin, familiar to Python developers for customization
REST API with Swagger docs for integrations
Available in over 20 languages
Includes built-in email marketing and campaign tools
cons: Requires Python/Django knowledge to install, customize, and maintain
Admin-framework-based UI is less polished than dedicated commercial CRM UIs
Self-hosting means you're responsible for updates and infrastructure

faqs.answer: Yes, it's free, open-source software with no license fees or per-user costs.

faqs.answer: It's built entirely on the Django Admin framework.

faqs.question: Does Django-CRM have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes REST API endpoints with interactive Swagger/OpenAPI documentation.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Django-CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's designed to be deployed on your own server or cloud infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: Free database management tool distributed as a single PHP file, supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, and more.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Adminer is a database management tool distributed as a single PHP file, created by Jakub Vrána starting in 2007 as a lighter-weight alternative to phpMyAdmin. Despite its minimal footprint, it supports a wide range of database engines, including MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, SQLite, MS SQL, Oracle, and CockroachDB, with additional systems such as Elasticsearch, MongoDB, Redis, and ClickHouse available via plugins.

From its single-file interface, Adminer covers the operations most database administrators need day to day: browsing and editing table structures, indexes, and foreign keys; managing views, stored procedures, triggers, and events; running custom SQL; and exporting data to SQL or CSV. It's released under the Apache License or GPL 2, free for both commercial and non-commercial use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Adminer is built for developers and database administrators who want a fast, no-install database GUI they can drop onto a server as a single file, rather than deploying and maintaining a heavier admin application like phpMyAdmin.

features.title: Single-file deployment
features.description: The entire tool is one PHP file, making it trivial to upload and run.

features.title: Multi-database support
features.description: Works with MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, SQLite, MS SQL, Oracle, and CockroachDB, with plugin support for more.

features.title: Schema and data editing
features.description: View and edit table structures, columns, indexes, foreign keys, views, triggers, and stored procedures.

features.title: SQL command execution
features.description: Run custom SQL queries directly from the interface.

features.title: Data export
features.description: Export data and structure to SQL or CSV formats.

features.title: Relationship visualization
features.description: Display database schemas with visualized table relationships.

pros: Free for commercial and non-commercial use
Single-file install makes deployment extremely simple
Broad database engine support, extensible via plugins
Available in 46 languages
Lighter and faster than phpMyAdmin for many workflows
cons: Interface is more minimal/utilitarian than modern GUI database clients
Requires PHP 5.3+ with sessions enabled on the host
Some database types (MongoDB, Redis, Elasticsearch) need separate plugins

faqs.question: Is Adminer free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free for both commercial and non-commercial use under the Apache License or GPL 2.

faqs.question: What databases does Adminer support?
faqs.answer: MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, SQLite, MS SQL, Oracle, and CockroachDB natively, with plugins for others like MongoDB and Redis.

faqs.question: How do I install Adminer?
faqs.answer: Download the single PHP file and place it on any PHP-enabled web server.

faqs.question: Is Adminer a replacement for phpMyAdmin?
faqs.answer: It's often used as a lighter-weight alternative, with the same site claiming a tidier interface and better performance.

use_cases.title: Quick database administration
use_cases.description: Browse, query, and edit databases without installing a full admin suite.

use_cases.title: Multi-engine environments
use_cases.description: Manage several different database engines from one lightweight tool.

use_cases.title: Data migration and export
use_cases.description: Export data and schema to SQL or CSV for backups or migrations.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Adminer is a free, open-source database management tool in a single PHP file, supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, and more.

enrichment.short_description: Database exploration and ERD tool for documenting and navigating large, complex databases, with a free self-hosted option.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Azimutt is an entity-relationship diagram and database exploration tool built for real-world databases that are large and messy rather than clean textbook schemas. It provides an interactive schema visualizer with incremental layout building, an AML domain-specific language for quickly sketching database designs, automated path-finding between tables, and database linting rules that flag common schema and runtime mistakes.

It connects to PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle, MariaDB, SQLite, MongoDB, Couchbase, and major cloud data warehouses such as AWS RDS/Aurora, Azure SQL, Google Cloud SQL, BigQuery, and Snowflake. The underlying application (Elixir/Phoenix backend, Elm/TypeScript frontend) is open source under the MIT license and can be self-hosted for free via Docker; hosted plans add collaboration, AI, and enterprise features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Azimutt is aimed at developers joining unfamiliar codebases, database administrators and architects, data analysts, and support/product teams who need to understand and document large or microservice-fragmented databases without deep prior knowledge of the schema.

features.title: Interactive schema visualization
features.description: Explore tables, columns, and relationships with customizable, incremental diagrams.

features.title: AML design language
features.description: A minimal DSL for quickly sketching or documenting database designs as code.

features.title: Automated path finding
features.description: Discover relationships and join paths between tables automatically.

features.title: Database linting
features.description: Rules that surface common schema and runtime mistakes.

features.title: Multi-database and warehouse support
features.description: Connect to PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, Snowflake, BigQuery, and more.

features.title: Documentation and notes
features.description: Attach notes to tables and columns, and save layouts for later reference.

features.title: Self-hosted or cloud
features.description: Run Azimutt for free via Docker, or use the hosted app with paid tiers.

pros: Free self-hosted option under an MIT license
Handles large, messy, real-world schemas well
Broad database and data-warehouse connector support
AML DSL enables quick schema documentation as code
No signup required to explore the free tier
cons: Free tier caps table count (10 tables in AML design) and saved projects (0)
AI and collaboration features are gated behind paid Team plans
Self-hosting requires running several services (Phoenix backend, Postgres, gateway)

faqs.question: Is Azimutt free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there's a free tier with no signup for exploration, and self-hosting via Docker is free under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What databases does Azimutt support?
faqs.answer: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle, MariaDB, SQLite, MongoDB, Couchbase, and cloud platforms like AWS RDS, Azure SQL, Google Cloud SQL, BigQuery, and Snowflake.

faqs.question: Can Azimutt be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, the full application is open source and can be self-hosted for free via Docker.

faqs.question: How much do paid Azimutt plans cost?
faqs.answer: As of research, Solo is €7/month and Team is €35/user/month (billed annually), with custom Enterprise pricing.

use_cases.title: Onboarding to a new codebase
use_cases.description: Quickly understand an unfamiliar or undocumented database schema.

use_cases.title: Database documentation
use_cases.description: Maintain living documentation of tables, columns, and relationships.

use_cases.title: Microservice schema mapping
use_cases.description: Trace relationships across fragmented, multi-service databases.

features: Up to 10 tables in AML design

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Azimutt is a database exploration and ERD tool for large, complex databases, with a free self-hosted option and paid team/enterprise plans.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source database DevSecOps platform for change management, access control, and audit across database types.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Bytebase is a database DevSecOps platform, built by former tech leads from Google Cloud SQL and Google's API infrastructure, that unifies how teams plan, review, and deploy database schema changes. It replaces manual scripts and ad-hoc processes with a structured change-management workflow, similar to how GitHub or GitLab manage code review for application code.

Beyond change management, Bytebase provides database access control with IAM-style policies, data masking, and just-in-time access exemptions, plus immutable audit logs for compliance visibility. It follows an open-core model: the MIT-licensed Community edition is free and self-hosted, supporting up to 20 users and 10 database instances, while Pro and Enterprise tiers add SSO, unlimited users, and advanced compliance features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Bytebase serves engineering, DBA, security, and compliance teams — particularly in regulated or larger organizations across financial services, technology, and gaming — that need controlled, auditable database change processes rather than direct production access.

features.title: Database change management
features.description: Plan, review, approve, and deploy schema changes through a structured workflow.

features.title: Access control
features.description: IAM-style policies, data masking, and just-in-time access exemptions.

features.title: Immutable audit logs
features.description: Track every database change and access event for compliance.

features.title: Multi-database support
features.description: Works across both OLTP and OLAP database types.

features.title: GitOps integration
features.description: Integrates with GitOps tools, identity providers, ITSM, and SIEM platforms.

features.title: Self-hosted or cloud deployment
features.description: Run the free Community edition self-hosted, or use hosted Pro/Enterprise plans.

pros: Free, MIT-licensed Community edition that's fully self-hosted
Structured, reviewable workflow for database schema changes
Immutable audit logging for compliance requirements
Broad integration support (GitOps, IdPs, ITSM, SIEM)
Backed by a dedicated team shipping regular releases
cons: Community edition caps at 20 users and 10 database instances
Pro plan is cloud-only; self-hosted Enterprise requires a yearly commitment
Advanced compliance features (SCIM, custom roles, HA) are Enterprise-only

faqs.question: Is Bytebase free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the MIT-licensed Community edition is free to self-host, supporting up to 20 users and 10 database instances.

faqs.question: How much does Bytebase Pro cost?
faqs.answer: As of research, Pro is $20 per user per month, cloud-hosted.

faqs.question: Can Bytebase be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community edition and Enterprise edition both support self-hosted deployment.

faqs.question: What does Bytebase do?
faqs.answer: It manages database schema change review/deployment, access control, and audit logging across multiple database types.

use_cases.title: Database change review
use_cases.description: Route schema changes through approval workflows instead of direct production access.

use_cases.title: Compliance and audit
use_cases.description: Maintain immutable logs of who changed or accessed what data, and when.

use_cases.title: Fine-grained database access control
use_cases.description: Apply IAM policies and data masking across environments.
use_cases.audience: Platform engineering teams in regulated industries

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bytebase is an open-source database DevSecOps platform for change management, access control, and audit, with a free self-hosted edition.
